1: "If a man marries a woman and she finds no favor with him... he shall write her a certificate of divorce." The divorce law that regulates but does not endorse divorce. Jesus cites this directly (Matthew 19:7–8; Mark 10:4): "Moses permitted it because of your hardness of hearts, but from the beginning it was not so." Christ does not abolish Deuteronomy 24:1 but reveals its function — it was a concession to sin, not a divine ideal. He restores the creation-order marriage the law could only regulate.
2: "Once she has left his house, she is free to marry another man." The divorced woman's freedom to remarry — a protection against limbo status. The law safeguards the woman's future against indefinite abandonment. Christ's concern for women left in vulnerable social positions (the widow of Nain, Luke 7:11–15; the woman bent double, Luke 13:10–17) extends this protective principle to those the social system has failed.
3: "But if her second husband also divorces her or dies..." The compounding tragedy of serial divorce. The law attends to the actual social consequences of divorce — not merely the legal acts but the human lives affected. Jesus's elevation of the marriage covenant to its creation-order permanence is motivated by the same concern: the law's accommodation to sin created precisely these tragic human situations that Christ's restoration of the covenant intends to prevent.
4: "Her first husband may not take her back after she has been with another man — that is detestable before the LORD." The remarriage prohibition protects against treating marriage as revolving-door convenience. Jeremiah 3:1 uses this exact law as an analogy for YHWH's relationship with Israel — "if a man divorces his wife and she goes away and becomes another man's wife, will he return to her? Would not the land be greatly defiled? But you have played the harlot with many lovers; yet return to me, says the LORD." YHWH's reconciliation with Israel transcends even the law's own provision — grace surpassing what law allows.
5: "When a man has recently married, he shall not serve in the army or be required for any public service. He shall be free at home for one year to bring happiness to the wife he has married." This law — protecting the first year of marriage — is alluded to in Jesus's parable of the great banquet (Luke 14:20): "I have just gotten married, and therefore I cannot come." The man uses the legitimate protection of Deuteronomy 24:5 as an excuse for not entering the kingdom. The law that rightly protected human love becomes an inadequate reason for rejecting the invitation of the coming King.
6: "Do not take a mill or a millstone as collateral for a loan — that would be seizing someone's livelihood." The protection of the basic means of life from debt seizure. Christ provides the true bread of life (John 6:35): "I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst." No creditor can seize what he gives. The law protects the means of earning bread; Christ gives the bread that needs no mill.
7: "If someone is caught kidnapping a fellow Israelite — treating him as a slave or selling him — that thief shall be put to death." The capital treatment of person-theft reflects that persons are not property. Christ's redemption liberates: "you were bought with a price; do not become slaves of men" (1 Corinthians 7:23). Paul transforms the slave-trade's own vocabulary — "bought at a price" — into gospel language: Christ's payment is the counter-transaction that undoes all human trafficking and exploitation of persons.
8: "In cases of skin disease, be very careful to follow exactly what the Levitical priests direct you." The instruction to work through the Levitical system for skin diseases. Jesus heals ten lepers and instructs them to show themselves to the priests (Luke 17:14) — "as they went, they were cleansed." He acts within the Levitical system while transcending it. The priest certified; Jesus heals. He is the great high priest (Hebrews 4:14) who offers a better cleansing than the Levitical diagnosis could provide.
9: "Remember what the LORD your God did to Miriam on the journey out of Egypt." Miriam's skin disease (Numbers 12:1–15) came from speaking against Moses. The memory-command recalls the consequence of undermining YHWH's chosen leader. The parallel to Christ: speaking against the Son is the ultimate sin (Matthew 12:32); the pattern of judgment for opposition to YHWH's prophet/leader is established and fulfilled in the final prophet-like-Moses, Jesus.
10: "When you make a loan to your neighbor, do not go into his home to take his pledge." The lender's restraint — respecting the borrower's domestic dignity. Christ's gentleness with the vulnerable is the positive version of this restraint: "a bruised reed he will not break, and a smoldering wick he will not quench" (Matthew 12:20, citing Isaiah 42:3). The Servant of YHWH does not force his way in or make demands that overwhelm the fragile.
11: "Stand outside and let him bring you his pledge." The borrower comes out to the lender — his home is his own, protected even in debt. The principle of seeking rather than forcing: the lender waits at the door. Christ stands at the door and knocks (Revelation 3:20): he does not break in, does not take by force; he waits for the one inside to open. The covenant's respect for human agency in receiving is fulfilled in Christ's respectful invitation.
12: "If he is poor, do not keep his pledge overnight." The poor man's cloak must be returned before sunset so he has something to sleep in. The covenant's active concern for the material welfare of the poor. James 2:15–17 applies the same logic: faith without practical care for the cold and hungry is dead. Christ's concern for the poor is constant (Matthew 5:3; Luke 4:18; Deuteronomy 6:20) — the Beatitudes and the inaugural sermon invoke the covenant's special concern for the materially destitute.
13: "Return his cloak at sunset so he can sleep warmly. He will bless you, and it will count as righteousness before the LORD your God." A small act of practical kindness "counted as righteousness" — the same gracious counting as in Genesis 15:6 (Abraham's faith counted as righteousness). The righteousness that Paul develops in Rom 4 and Gal 3 — faith counted as righteousness — operates within a world where YHWH graciously counts acts of trust and love as covenant standing. Christ is the ground of all such counting (2 Corinthians 5:21).
14: "Do not mistreat a poor and needy hired worker — whether a fellow Israelite or a foreigner living in one of your towns." The covenant's protection extends to the foreigner laborer — not just the citizen. Jesus's parable of the workers in the vineyard (Matthew 20:1–16) operates within this labor-protection context: the landowner pays wages at sunset (as Deuteronomy 24:15 requires) and pays generously beyond what was contractually owed. The grace of the kingdom exceeds even the law's provision for workers.
15: "Pay his wages the same day, before sunset — he is poor and counting on it. If you withhold them and he cries out to the LORD, you will be guilty of sin." James 5:4 echoes this exactly: "the wages you failed to pay your workers who mowed your fields are crying out against you." Christ as the eschatological judge (Matthew 25:31–46) hears the cry of the unpaid worker and the hungry and the naked — the Deuteronomic categories of covenant obligation become the criteria of the final judgment.
16: "Parents shall not be put to death for the sins of their children, and children shall not be put to death for the sins of their parents. Each person shall be executed only for his own sin." Individual accountability before the law. Ezekiel 18 develops this more fully. The principle creates the conceptual space for Christ's substitutionary death: he who is entirely innocent voluntarily takes the death that should fall on the guilty (2 Corinthians 5:21). Individual accountability means Christ's death is not mere shared communal guilt — it is his specific, deliberate assumption of the specific, individual guilt of those he represents.
17: "Do not deprive the foreigner or the fatherless of justice, or take a widow's garment as collateral." The triad — foreigner, fatherless, widow — is the covenant's shorthand for the structurally vulnerable. Jesus is the advocate of all three: Luke 4:18 ("good news to the poor, liberty to the oppressed"), John 14:18 ("I will not leave you as orphans"), and Luke 18:1–8 (the parable of the persistent widow). He takes up the cause of each category the law identified as requiring special protection.
18: "Remember that you were a slave in Egypt and the LORD your God redeemed you. That is why I give you this command." The memory of redemption motivates present generosity toward the vulnerable. Paul applies the same logic: "be kind and compassionate to one another, forgiving each other, just as in Christ God forgave you" (Ephesians 4:32). The shape of the imperative follows the shape of the indicative: what Christ has done to us becomes what we do toward others.
19: "When you harvest your field and leave a sheaf behind, do not go back for it — leave it for the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow." The gleaning law — intentional incompleteness as covenant provision for the poor. This is the law Boaz applied when Ruth gleaned in his field (Ruth 2). Ruth the Moabite, a foreigner and widow, gleans under this law and enters the Messianic lineage (Matthew 1:5). The gleaning law is not incidentally but structurally connected to the ancestry of Christ.
20: "When you have beaten the olives off your trees, do not go back for what is left — it belongs to the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow." The same principle extended to orchards. The deliberate leaving of surplus for the poor is a covenant practice of generosity built into the agricultural system. The new covenant equivalent is the cheerful giver who does not count the cost (2 Corinthians 9:7): what the law required structurally, the Spirit produces willingly in those who have received grace.
21: "When you harvest your vineyard, do not go back for every last bunch — leave the remainder for the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow." The vineyard gleaning law. The vineyard is the OT's most frequent metaphor for Israel (Isaiah 5:1–7; Psalms 80:8–16). Jesus takes up the same metaphor: "I am the true vine" (John 15:1) and the parables of the vineyard owner (Matthew 20:1–16; Deuteronomy 21:33–46). The gleaning law embedded in the vineyard points to the new covenant's abundance made available to the Gentile "foreigners" who come to glean in Christ's vineyard.
22: "Remember that you were a slave in Egypt. That is why I am giving you this command." The Exodus memory as the motivational ground of all covenant generosity. The NT equivalent: "God shows his love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us" (Romans 5:8). The memory of what God did for us when we were enslaved and helpless motivates the same generosity toward others in our community. The Exodus memory is expanded by the cross-memory in the new covenant.
